name: MeToo
issue: Systemic sexual harassment and assault normalized across institutions worldwide.

ncrs.rationale: Cultural tipping point achieved with 19M tweets in 24 hours. High-profile firings created visible benefits. Women across demographics participated. Hashtag format lowered barriers to participation while Tarana Burke and celebrity amplifiers maintained pressure.

trigger: Ronan Farrow's Harvey Weinstein exposé in New Yorker/NYT (Oct 5-10, 2017) + Alyssa Milano's tweet inviting survivors to reply "Me too" — Oct 15, 2017.
outcome: 19 million tweets in 24 hours. Dozens of high-profile firings and resignations within weeks. Legislative changes on NDAs in 12+ US states and several countries. 2022 Speak Out Act federally. Ongoing institutional reform in Hollywood, media, tech. Harvey Weinstein convicted 2020.
intervention_levers: Digital participation infrastructure (hashtag format), celebrity amplification, investigative journalism partnerships, legislative advocacy on NDA reform, workplace policy reform campaigns, survivor support networks.

name: Black Lives Matter — The 2020 Tipping
issue: Police violence against Black Americans as state-sanctioned racial violence requiring systemic dismantling.

ncrs.rationale: Largest protest movement in American history with 15-26M participants. Seven years of infrastructure-building preceded 2020. COVID lockdowns freed people to protest. Video evidence eliminated ambiguity. Global spread to 60 countries demonstrated universal resonance.

trigger: Bystander video of Derek Chauvin kneeling on George Floyd's neck for 8 minutes 46 seconds, May 25, 2020. Multiple camera angles. Zero ambiguity. Officers did not know they were filmed.
outcome: 15-26 million US participants (largest protest in American history). 4,000+ protests in 60 countries. $10.6 billion pledged to racial justice orgs. Policing reform debates in hundreds of jurisdictions. Dozens of statues removed. Long-term: polarized; many reforms reversed. But cultural shift permanent.
intervention_levers: Video documentation infrastructure, decentralized organizing, corporate pressure campaigns, electoral mobilization, police reform legislation, community investment advocacy, coalition-building across racial lines.

name: Plastic Straw / Sea Turtle
issue: Single-use plastic's impact on marine ecosystems; accountability for plastic producers.

ncrs.rationale: Viral video made abstract harm visceral. Low-cost consumer action (refusing straws) made participation easy. Corporate adoption (Starbucks, McDonald's) normalized change. However, straws represent 0.025% of ocean plastic — narrative win exceeded material impact.

trigger: 2015 video of marine biologist removing plastic straw from sea turtle nostril — 28 million views. Small, specific, physically familiar object. Undeniable pain.
outcome: Plastic straw bans in 70+ countries and thousands of cities. Major corporations (Starbucks, McDonald's) eliminated straws. But: straws = 0.025% of ocean plastic by weight. Narrative win exceeded material win. However, it demonstrated accountability framing for larger campaigns.
intervention_levers: Viral video distribution, corporate campaigns targeting consumer brands, municipal policy advocacy, restaurant industry partnerships, alternative product development, broader plastics treaty advocacy.
slogans: Ditch the straw, save a turtle

name: Fridays for Future
issue: Government inaction on climate change in the face of scientific consensus and escalating disasters.

ncrs.rationale: 6 million participants in September 2019 global strike. Youth framing created moral authority. Greta's persistence absorbed enormous personal cost. Reframed truancy as moral necessity. Climate emergency declarations followed but policy implementation lagged.

trigger: Greta Thunberg's solo strike outside Swedish parliament, August 20, 2018. The global tip: September 20, 2019 Global Climate Strike timed to UN Climate Action Summit — 6 million people in 185 countries.
outcome: 6 million participants globally, Sept 2019. "Climate emergency" declarations by 2,000+ jurisdictions. Divestment acceleration. New Green Deal proposals advanced in multiple countries. Post-COVID stall significant. Long-term: normalized youth as climate political actors.
intervention_levers: Youth organizing networks, school strike coordination, social media amplification, international summit timing, climate litigation support, divestment campaigns, coalition with scientists and educators.
slogans: You're never too small to make a difference

name: Standing Rock / #NoDAPL
issue: Dakota Access Pipeline threatening Standing Rock Sioux water supply and violating treaty rights.

ncrs.rationale: Indigenous-led resistance created powerful imagery echoing civil rights era. Water protectors faced violence but maintained nonviolent stance. Obama halted pipeline but Trump reversed. Movement built lasting indigenous-environmental alliance infrastructure.

trigger: September 3-4, 2016: private security dogs attacking water protectors. Video footage. The imagery looked exactly like civil rights-era footage — water cannons, dogs, peaceful protesters.
outcome: Obama halted pipeline (Dec 2016). Trump approved it (Jan 2017). Pipeline completed. Cultural impact lasting: massive growth in indigenous rights consciousness, "land back" movement acceleration, alliance-building between indigenous and environmental movements that shaped subsequent campaigns.
intervention_levers: Indigenous sovereignty framing, water-as-sacred narrative, alliance-building with environmental groups, legal challenges on treaty rights, divestment campaigns targeting pipeline financiers, documentary filmmaking.

name: Marriage Equality — The Long Arc
issue: Legal recognition of same-sex marriage; LGBTQ+ equality under law.

ncrs.rationale: Complete legal victory achieved through decades of deliberate engineering. Public support crossed 50% by 2011. Coming-out strategy made support visible. Cultural tipping preceded legal tipping. Organizations like HRC, Lambda Legal provided persistent leadership for 22+ years.

trigger: Supreme Court's Windsor decision (2013) gutting DOMA, then Obergefell arguments (2015) as public opinion had already crossed 50% support around 2011.
outcome: Obergefell v. Hodges — nationwide marriage equality June 2015. Public support went from 27% (1996) to 62% (2015). Legal tipping followed cultural tipping by ~3 years.
intervention_levers: Strategic litigation pathways, coming-out as political strategy, cultural representation in media, state-by-state legislative wins, polling and message testing, religious coalition-building, corporate ally cultivation.

name: Chile Estallido Social
issue: Accumulated economic inequality, privatized infrastructure, and elite impunity in Chile's neoliberal model.

ncrs.rationale: 30 years of accumulated grievance detonated by 4-cent fare increase. Massive mobilization forced constitutional convention. However, two referendums rejected new constitutions. Movement identified the problem but couldn't consolidate institutional change.

trigger: October 18, 2019: subway fare increase of 30 pesos ($0.04). Coordinated student fare evasion was met with military force. State of emergency declared. Images of military in streets for first time since Pinochet.
outcome: Government agreed to constitutional convention — historic first in Chile. New constitution drafted 2021-2022 but rejected by referendum (July 2022). Second process also failed (Dec 2023). Piñera made minor concessions. Structural change incomplete but political landscape permanently altered; Boric government elected.
intervention_levers: Student organizing networks, cacerolazos (pot-banging) as participation format, feminist movement integration, constitutional assembly process, international solidarity, cultural production (Las Tesis choreography went global).
slogans: Hasta Que La Dignidad Se Haga Costumbre
El pueblo unido jamás será vencido

name: EndSARS Nigeria
issue: Police brutality by Special Anti-Robbery Squad (SARS), systematic extortion and torture of young Nigerians.

ncrs.rationale: Youth-led movement achieved SARS disbandment in 8 days. However, Lekki Toll Gate massacre demonstrated state's willingness to use lethal force. SWAT rebrand without reform. Movement shifted political consciousness of Nigerian youth permanently.

trigger: October 3, 2020: video of SARS officers killing a young man in Delta State went viral on Twitter. Simultaneously, COVID-19 had created a generation of young people with nothing to lose.
outcome: SARS officially disbanded October 11, 2020. However: Lekki Toll Gate massacre October 20 — military opened fire on protesters. Conflicting accounts of deaths. Government announced Special Weapons and Tactics Team (SWAT) to replace SARS — widely seen as rebrand without reform. Medium-term: significant shift in Nigerian youth political engagement.
intervention_levers: Twitter organizing, diaspora amplification, celebrity support, cryptocurrency donations (after bank freezes), documentary filmmaking, electoral mobilization for subsequent elections.

name: India Farmers' Protest
issue: Three Farm Acts deregulating agricultural markets and threatening minimum support prices for 250 million farmers.

ncrs.rationale: Sustained 13-month protest achieved unprecedented government capitulation. Farmer unions provided organizational backbone. Clear economic threat made benefits visible. Modi's personal announcement of repeal demonstrated complete victory. One of most successful movements in recent Indian history.

trigger: November 26, 2020 "Dilli Chalo" (March to Delhi) — farmers attempted to march on capital. Police met them with tear gas and water cannons (in winter). Images of elderly Sikh farmers being tear-gassed created global outrage.
outcome: November 19, 2021: PM Modi announced repeal of all three Farm Acts on national television. Unprecedented reversal. One of the most significant government capitulations in Indian democratic history. The movement won — fully.
intervention_levers: Farmer union coordination, tractor rallies, border encampment infrastructure, Sikh community networks, diaspora pressure, international media coverage, Rihanna tweet amplification, sustained nonviolent discipline.

name: South Korea Candlelight Revolution
issue: President Park Geun-hye's corruption and influence by Choi Soon-sil cult figure; democratic accountability.

ncrs.rationale: Peaceful protests achieved constitutional impeachment. Weekly Saturday marches maintained pressure without violence. Court system functioned to uphold democracy. Beautiful candlelight aesthetic created images impossible to delegitimize. Model democratic accountability movement.

trigger: October 2016: revelations that Park had been sharing classified state documents with Choi Soon-sil, a shamanistic cult leader with no government role, who had been effectively co-governing South Korea.
outcome: Park impeached December 9, 2016. Constitutional Court upheld impeachment March 2017. Arrested and convicted 2018. Moon Jae-in elected. Democracy functioned. One of the cleanest examples of a peaceful democratic accountability movement succeeding.
intervention_levers: Weekly protest format, candlelight aesthetic, Constitutional Court process, investigative journalism, labor union participation, student networks, K-pop fan organizing (precursor to later BTS activism).

name: Argentina Abortion / Green Wave
issue: Legalization of abortion in Argentina; reproductive rights across Latin America.

ncrs.rationale: Green handkerchief created instant visual identity crossing borders. 38 years of organizing built to 2020 victory. Ripple effect to Mexico and Colombia showed regional contagion. Feminist infrastructure from NiUnaMenos provided organizational backbone.

trigger: December 30, 2020: Argentine Congress passed the Voluntary Interruption of Pregnancy Law at 4am — 38 years after the return of democracy. The vote itself was the trigger for global celebration.
outcome: Abortion legal in Argentina up to 14 weeks. Ripple effect: Mexico Supreme Court ruled abortion bans unconstitutional (2021), Colombia legalized abortion up to 24 weeks (2022). Green wave transformed regional policy landscape.
intervention_levers: Visual symbol (green handkerchief), NiUnaMenos infrastructure, legislative lobbying, testimony from health workers, regional coordination, celebrity support, street presence during votes.
slogans: Educación sexual para decidir, anticonceptivos para no abortar, aborto legal para no morir

name: NiUnaMenos — Anti-Femicide
issue: Epidemic of femicide (gender-based murder of women) treated as private tragedy rather than systemic violence.

ncrs.rationale: Made invisible pattern visible by naming femicide as systemic crime. Built organizational infrastructure that produced Green Wave victory. Cross-border spread throughout Latin America. Artists, journalists, and activists created enduring movement architecture.

trigger: June 3, 2015: Massive march in Buenos Aires after the murder of 14-year-old Chiara Páez, found buried in the backyard of her boyfriend's home while pregnant. Artists, journalists, and activists organized.
outcome: Created the framework for femicide as a named, systemic crime rather than domestic tragedy. Influenced femicide legislation in Argentina (2012 law strengthened) and throughout region. Built the organizational infrastructure that produced the Green Wave abortion legalization movement. Permanent shift in how gender violence is discussed.
intervention_levers: Counting femicides (making the pattern visible), journalist networks, artist collectives, annual march dates, hashtag organizing, legislative advocacy, regional coordination through feminist networks.
slogans: Si Tocan a Una Nos Tocan a Todas
Nos queremos vivas, libres y sin miedo

name: Fees Must Fall
issue: Rising university fees and inadequate transformation of apartheid-era educational inequalities.

ncrs.rationale: Connected economic grievance (fees) to symbolic transformation (Rhodes statue). Won immediate zero fee increase and later fee-free education for lowest-income students. However, structural transformation of universities incomplete.

trigger: October 14, 2015: Wits University announced 10.5% fee increase. Student council vice-president stormed administration building. Within 24 hours, protests spread across all major universities. October 23: Parliament surrounded.
outcome: President Zuma announced zero fee increase for 2016. Short-term victory. Government announced fee-free higher education for poor and working-class students (2017). Longer term: implementation uneven; structural transformation of university character incomplete. But fee-free education for lowest-income achieved.
intervention_levers: Cross-campus coordination, social media organizing, occupation tactics, linking fees to decolonization narrative, international solidarity, alumni networks, parliamentary pressure.
slogans: Born free, but still in chains

name: Sudan Revolution
issue: Overthrow of Omar al-Bashir's 30-year authoritarian regime amid economic collapse and bread shortages.

ncrs.rationale: Ended 30-year dictatorship through sustained mobilization. Alaa Salah's iconic image went globally viral. However, June 3 sit-in massacre and subsequent military coup ended democratic transition. Revolution succeeded then was betrayed.

trigger: December 19, 2018: Price of bread tripled overnight. Protests in Atbara — teachers and students. Security forces killed protesters. Protests spread within days to Khartoum.
outcome: Al-Bashir ousted by military April 11, 2019 — ending 30-year rule. Transitional civilian-military council established. Sit-in massacred June 3, 2019 (60-100+ killed). International pressure secured civilian-military "Sovereignty Council." Democratic transition stalled — 2021 military coup ended democratic hopes.
intervention_levers: Professional associations (doctors, lawyers), neighborhood committees, women's leadership, sit-in infrastructure, international diplomatic pressure, diaspora coordination, documentation of massacres.

name: Malawi Election Protests
issue: Fraudulent 2019 presidential election — tipp-ex (correction fluid) used to alter election results.

ncrs.rationale: Physical evidence (tipp-ex corrections) was unambiguous. Constitutional Court annulled election — first in African history. Fresh elections held and respected. Landmark democratic accountability precedent for the continent.

trigger: Tallying sheets with tipp-ex corrections visible — evidence of systematic result alteration. Photographs of the altered sheets circulated on social media and were unmistakable.
outcome: Constitutional Court annulled election February 2020. Fresh elections held June 2020. Mutharika lost; Lazarus Chakwera won. First time in African history a court annulled a presidential election and the result was respected. Landmark democratic accountability precedent.
intervention_levers: Photographic evidence documentation, civil society election monitoring, Constitutional Court litigation, church leadership, regional diplomatic pressure, sustained peaceful protests.

name: Zimbabwe: Fall of Mugabe
issue: End of Robert Mugabe's 37-year rule amid economic collapse and succession conflict.

ncrs.rationale: Military coup enabled by popular approval removed Mugabe, but Mnangagwa continued authoritarian governance. Popular celebration masked elite power transfer. Not a democratic revolution — a factional victory within the ruling party.

trigger: November 14, 2017: Military seized state broadcaster ZBC and announced "not a coup" (immediately recognizable as a coup). Generals placed Mugabe under house arrest. Rather than generating resistance, population celebrated.
outcome: Mugabe resigned November 21, 2017 (after impeachment proceedings began). Emmerson Mnangagwa assumed power. This was a military/elite removal enabled by popular approval — not a democratic revolution. Mnangagwa has continued authoritarian governance; political prisoners, electoral fraud continued.
intervention_levers: Limited — this was primarily an elite-driven transition. Civil society played supporting role. International recognition of new government provided legitimacy. Future interventions would need to target ZANU-PF factionalism or economic pressure.

name: Green Belt Movement — Kenya
issue: Deforestation, land degradation, and women's disempowerment in Kenya.

ncrs.rationale: 27 years of relentless work before Nobel Prize recognition. 51 million trees planted. Model replicated in 30+ countries. Wangari Maathai absorbed enormous personal cost. Constitutional reforms included environmental rights. Demonstrated power of persistent leadership.

trigger: The 2004 Nobel Peace Prize — first time Peace Prize awarded to an environmental activist. This was the trigger that transformed the movement's global visibility and legitimized the connection between environment and peace.
outcome: 51 million trees planted. Constitutional reforms in Kenya that included environmental rights. Maathai appointed Assistant Minister for Environment. The movement created a model (women + trees + democracy) replicated in 30+ countries globally. 2005 death didn't end movement; it became a permanent institution.
intervention_levers: Women's groups as organizing base, tree planting as participatory action, environmental education, civic education linking environment to democracy, international awards and recognition, replication in other countries.
slogans: When We Plant Trees, We Plant Seeds of Peace

name: Kenya Maandamano / Finance Bill Protests
issue: Finance Bill 2023 proposing new taxes amid cost of living crisis; broader government accountability.

ncrs.rationale: Gen Z TikTok organizing achieved Finance Bill withdrawal in 8 days. Police violence backfired, expanding mobilization. First major capitulation of Ruto government. Demonstrated new organizing infrastructure through social media native generation.

trigger: June 18, 2024: Kenyan Gen Z organizers called for protest via TikTok and X against Finance Bill. What was anticipated as a small protest became massive when police used live ammunition — killing 3, wounding many.
outcome: Ruto signed withdrawal of Finance Bill June 26, 2024. First major capitulation of his government. Subsequent protests demanded broader government accountability. Cabinet reshuffled. Movement continued through 2024 with ongoing accountability demands.
intervention_levers: TikTok organizing, meme culture, decentralized coordination, diaspora amplification, legal observer networks, documentation of police violence, occupying parliament building.
slogans: We are Gen Z, we are fearless

name: Burkina Faso Popular Uprising
issue: President Blaise Compaoré's attempt to extend his 27-year rule through constitutional amendment.

ncrs.rationale: Stormed parliament and forced president to flee within 24 hours. First non-electoral transfer in West Africa since 1980s. However, 2022 military coup ended democratic experiment — demonstrating fragility of revolution without institutional consolidation.

trigger: October 30, 2014: Parliamentary session to vote on constitutional amendment. Thousands stormed the building. Parliament set on fire. Ruling party headquarters burned. Security forces pulled back.
outcome: Compaoré fled to Ivory Coast within 24 hours. First non-electoral transfer of power in West Africa since 1980s that was recognized internationally as legitimate. Transition government formed. However: military coup 2022 ended democratic experiment — demonstrating the fragility of revolution without institutional consolidation.
intervention_levers: Youth movements (Balai Citoyen), civil society coalitions, musician leadership (Smockey), regional diplomatic engagement, transition government design, constitutional reform process.
slogans: Plus Rien Ne Sera Comme Avant

name: Ethiopia Oromo Protests / Abiy Ahmed Rise
issue: Marginalization of Oromo people, land grabs around Addis Ababa, ethnic discrimination under TPLF-dominated EPRDF.

ncrs.rationale: Sustained pressure forced EPRDF reforms and Abiy Ahmed's rise. Initial reforms promising — political prisoners released, Eritrea peace deal, Nobel Prize. However, Tigray War demonstrated that ethnic grievances were not resolved, only reconfigured.

trigger: November 2015: students and farmers protest the Master Plan. Security forces kill protesters. The Irreecha religious festival massacre (October 2016) — 50+ killed in stampede triggered by security forces — amplified outrage.
outcome: State of emergency declared; internet shutdowns. But the sustained pressure forced EPRDF to announce reforms. Abiy Ahmed (Oromo reform candidate) became PM April 2018 — released political prisoners, lifted Ethiopia from US terrorist designation, initiated Nobel-winning Eritrea peace deal. However: Tigray War 2020-2022 killed hundreds of thousands.
intervention_levers: Qeerroo youth networks, diaspora coordination, religious occasions as mobilization points, international human rights pressure, internal EPRDF reform faction support, regional diplomatic engagement.

name: Ghana #FixTheCountry
issue: Infrastructure failures, joblessness, and governance dysfunction under Akufo-Addo government.

ncrs.rationale: Demonstrated new form of Ghanaian youth activism connecting to diaspora. Legal battle over right to protest became powerful story. Limited structural change but significant shift in political discourse and youth engagement.

trigger: Flooding in Accra, May 2021, killing 6 and destroying property — hashtag #FixTheCountry trended nationally and proposed a protest. Police initially obtained court injunction blocking protest.
outcome: Protests occurred; government announced infrastructure investment. Movement successfully demonstrated a new form of Ghanaian youth activism connecting to diaspora. Limited structural change but significant shift in political discourse and youth political engagement.
intervention_levers: Social media organizing, diaspora networks, legal challenges to protest bans, youth entrepreneurship framing, accountability journalism, connecting specific infrastructure failures to governance.
slogans: The System Is Working Against Us

name: Morocco Hirak Rif Movement
issue: Marginalization of the Rif region, lack of investment, death of a fish vendor in a garbage truck.

ncrs.rationale: Grotesque specific death embodied decades of structural neglect. However, state repression was severe — Zefzafi sentenced to 20 years, 50+ activists imprisoned. Without international institutional pressure, movement was crushed despite visual power.

trigger: October 28, 2016: Mouhcine Fikri, a fish vendor, crushed to death in garbage truck — he had jumped in to retrieve his confiscated swordfish after police ordered it destroyed. Video went viral.
outcome: Zefzafi arrested May 2017; sentenced to 20 years. 50+ activists imprisoned. Mass trials 2018. Movement suppressed. International human rights organizations condemned sentences. Limited regional investment announcements but no structural change and severe repression of leadership.
intervention_levers: International human rights pressure, diaspora mobilization, documentation of repression, regional grievance articulation, connecting to broader Moroccan reform movements, sustained attention on political prisoners.

name: Mali Protests / IBK Resignation
issue: President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta's corruption, deteriorating security, and election fraud.

ncrs.rationale: Successfully removed IBK from power. However, military junta — not civilian protesters — took power. Junta later expelled French forces and pivoted to Russia/Wagner Group. Revolution's outcome was authoritarian substitution, not democratic transition.

trigger: June 5, 2020: "June 5 Movement" emerged after Supreme Court reinstated contested election results. Massive protests in Bamako. Protesters attacked and looted parliament on July 10-11.
outcome: IBK resigned August 18, 2020 following military coup. ECOWAS imposed sanctions. Military junta — not civilian protesters — took power. Junta later expelled French forces and pivoted to Russia/Wagner Group. Revolution's outcome was authoritarian substitution, not democratic transition.
intervention_levers: Religious leader coordination (Imam Dicko), regional diplomatic pressure, democratic transition design, civil society capacity building, security sector reform.

name: South Africa Marikana and the Platinum Belt
issue: Rock drill operators' wage demands at Lonmin platinum mine; decades of labor exploitation and inequality.

ncrs.rationale: Massacre permanently damaged ANC credibility among working class. Led to EFF founding. However, no criminal convictions of police. Ramaphosa (Lonmin board member) became president despite controversy. Workers received partial wage increase but structural exploitation continued.

trigger: August 16, 2012: South African police shot and killed 34 striking miners in what became known as the Marikana Massacre. The first large-scale state killing of Black workers since the end of apartheid.
outcome: Workers received partial wage increase. But broader: ANC credibility among working class permanently damaged. Economic Freedom Fighters (EFF) founded 2013. Lonmin eventually closed, bought by Sibanye. No criminal convictions of police. Cyril Ramaphosa (Lonmin board member during massacre) faced ongoing political liability.
intervention_levers: Documentary filmmaking, commission of inquiry pressure, political party formation (EFF), international mining industry pressure, worker organizing beyond traditional unions, connecting to broader inequality narrative.

name: Liberia Peace Women / Leymah Gbowee
issue: Ending Charles Taylor's civil war; women's mobilization for peace.

ncrs.rationale: Women's action directly changed civil war trajectory. Culturally specific moral authority (threatened disrobing) broke negotiation impasse. Ellen Johnson Sirleaf elected first female African head of state. Clearest case of direct nonviolent action changing violent conflict.

trigger: Peace talks in Ghana, July 2003: Women's delegation surrounded the building. When peace talks stalled, they sat in the hall and declared they would not leave. Gbowee threatened to invoke "bad luck" through disrobing if men did not make peace.
outcome: Taylor forced from power. Peace deal signed. Ellen Johnson Sirleaf elected (first female African head of state). The Women of Liberia Mass Action for Peace changed a civil war's outcome — one of the clearest cases of direct nonviolent action changing a violent conflict's trajectory.
intervention_levers: Women's organizing across religious lines (Christian-Muslim), culturally specific moral authority, physical presence at negotiations, international media coverage, post-conflict political organizing, Nobel Prize amplification.
slogans: We Want Peace, No More War
Pray the Devil Back to Hell

name: Taiwan Sunflower Movement
issue: Cross-Strait Services Trade Agreement with China passed in improper legislative review — concerns about economic dependence on Beijing.

ncrs.rationale: Occupied parliament for 24 days. Agreement shelved indefinitely. Movement catalyzed generation of civic engagement — leaders now in government. DPP won 2016 elections partly due to movement momentum. Identity defense against China resonated broadly.

trigger: March 18, 2014: Students stormed and occupied the Legislative Yuan (parliament building) after the committee chair approved the agreement without full review in under a minute. The procedural move was the trigger.
outcome: Agreement indefinitely shelved. Ma government agreed to legislation requiring democratic oversight of cross-strait agreements. Long-term: DPP won 2016 elections; agreement never ratified. Movement catalyzed a generation of Taiwan civic engagement including some leaders now in government.
intervention_levers: Parliament occupation tactics, live-streaming infrastructure, academic support networks, identity framing (Taiwan vs. China), procedural justice narrative, legislative reform advocacy.
slogans: 自己的國家自己救 (Save our own country ourselves)

name: Sri Lanka Aragalaya — "Gota Go Gama"
issue: Economic collapse under Rajapaksa government — foreign reserves exhausted, no fuel, no medicine, 13-hour daily power cuts.

ncrs.rationale: Economic collapse made grievance universal. President fled country. Protest camp as parallel society demonstrated alternative governance. Cross-ethnic, cross-religious coalition unprecedented in Sri Lankan history.

trigger: March 31, 2022: People spontaneously gathered outside President Gotabaya Rajapaksa's private residence in an upscale Colombo suburb — not organized by any political party. Curfew imposed; protesters refused to leave.
outcome: Gotabaya Rajapaksa fled to Maldives, then Singapore, resigned July 14, 2022. Mahinda Rajapaksa (PM brother) already resigned. Historic: an economic collapse produced a successful peaceful removal of a government. New government negotiated IMF deal. Root causes (family governance, debt) partially addressed.
intervention_levers: Protest camp infrastructure (library, food, healthcare), cross-ethnic coalition-building, social media documentation, diaspora support, occupying presidential residence symbolically, IMF negotiation engagement.

name: Thailand Student Democracy Movement
issue: Demands for democratic reform, reduction of military's political role, and unprecedented demands for monarchy reform.

ncrs.rationale: Broke the taboo on monarchy criticism — unprecedented in Thai history. However, lèse-majesté prosecutions suppressed movement. Move Forward party won 2023 elections but was blocked from government; leader banned from politics. Creative visual tactics insufficient against state legal power.

trigger: Future Forward dissolution sparked university "flash mobs" (quick protest gatherings). COVID-19 suppressed them temporarily. Reform party dissolution + pandemic economic pain + King Vajiralongkorn's spending during COVID created the compound crisis.
outcome: Movement suppressed through royal defamation (lèse-majesté) prosecutions — hundreds charged. No structural reforms. Prayuth government survived. 2023 elections: progressive Move Forward party won largest share of parliament but was blocked from forming government; its leader subsequently banned from politics.
intervention_levers: International pressure on lèse-majesté, diaspora organizing, legal defense networks, creative cultural production, electoral organizing for Move Forward, regional democracy solidarity.
slogans: ปฏิรูปสถาบัน (Reform the Institution)

name: Japan Anti-Nuclear Movement
issue: Nuclear power safety following Fukushima Daiichi triple meltdown; energy policy democracy.

ncrs.rationale: All 50 reactors shut down by 2013. Discovery of government lies about radiation created unique emotional response in high-trust society. However, Abe government gradually restarted reactors after 2015. Movement reduced but didn't eliminate nuclear role.

trigger: Government's inadequate transparency about radiation levels and coverup attempts. Multiple lies: radiation levels downplayed, exclusion zone too small, food safety data suppressed. The specific trigger: revelation of systematic information betrayal by TEPCO and government.
outcome: By 2013, all 50 operational nuclear reactors shut down. "Zero nuclear" briefly achieved. But: Abe government gradually restarted reactors after 2015 despite continued public opposition. As of 2024, ~12 reactors operational. Movement reduced nuclear role but didn't eliminate it.
intervention_levers: Weekly Friday protests at PM residence, scientific expertise mobilization, compensation litigation, renewable energy advocacy, electoral pressure, exposing TEPCO cover-ups, local referendum campaigns.

name: Pakistan Aurat March
issue: Gender-based violence, women's rights, and feminist visibility in a socially conservative society.

ncrs.rationale: Annual march continues despite threats and backlash. No specific legislation but significantly shifted discourse. Conservative backlash actually amplified reach. Movement demonstrates how opposition can serve as better amplification than approval.

trigger: March 8, 2018: First Aurat March (Women's March). But the real tipping trigger was 2019 — counter-protests by religious conservatives and threats of violence, combined with a poster that said "Mera jism meri marzi" (My body my choice), created a national debate that amplified the movement far beyond its original reach.
outcome: The march continues annually; it has not produced specific legislation but has significantly shifted the discourse around women's rights in Pakistan. The legal status of "My body my choice" debate in Pakistani courts represents a permanent political opening. Movement demonstrates how conservative backlash can serve as amplification.
intervention_levers: Annual march coordination, social media documentation, legal defense against blasphemy accusations, international solidarity, artistic expression, connecting to global feminist movements while maintaining local framing.
slogans: Mera Jism Meri Marzi (My body, my choice)
Khud khana garam karo (Heat your own food)

name: Myanmar Civil Disobedience Movement
issue: Opposition to February 1, 2021 military coup overthrowing elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi.

ncrs.rationale: CDM had near-universal participation initially. However, military responded with lethal force — hundreds killed, thousands arrested. Armed resistance formed. Civil war ongoing. Demonstrates that even high-scoring movements lose when opponents control lethal violence without accountability.

trigger: The coup itself. Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM) launched within days — striking doctors, nurses, teachers, and civil servants refusing to work for the junta.
outcome: Junta responded with increasingly lethal force. By March-April 2021: hundreds killed; thousands arrested. Protests suppressed in cities. Armed resistance (People's Defence Force) formed. Civil war ongoing as of 2025. International community imposed sanctions but not sufficient to change junta behavior.
intervention_levers: International sanctions, arms embargo enforcement, diaspora coordination, humanitarian support, refugee support, recognition of shadow government (NUG), corporate pressure on junta-linked businesses.

name: Philippines: Killing of Environmental Activists / Anti-EJK Movement
issue: Extrajudicial killings under Duterte's "War on Drugs" and systematic assassination of environmental defenders.

ncrs.rationale: No single trigger produced domestic tipping — volume of killings numbed audience. ICC opened investigation. De Lima released after 6 years. Killings slowed after Duterte left office but no criminal accountability achieved.

trigger: No single trigger produced a domestic tipping — the volume of killings numbed the domestic audience. International ICC complaint filed in 2021 was a partial institutional trigger.
outcome: Duterte did not finish term but was constitutionally barred from re-election; succeeded by Marcos Jr. ICC opened preliminary investigation. Killings slowed after Duterte left office. Environmental defender killings continued. De Lima released 2023 after 6 years imprisonment. No criminal accountability for thousands of deaths.
intervention_levers: ICC litigation, international human rights documentation, diaspora organizing, protecting specific high-profile cases (De Lima), electoral organizing against Duterte allies, environmental defender protection networks.

name: Indonesia: Anti-Corruption KPK Defense
issue: Government's weakening of the KPK (Corruption Eradication Commission) through legislative amendments.

ncrs.rationale: Students won some battles — some bills withdrawn. But KPK revisions passed; new supervisory board hobbled the body. KPK conviction rate dropped dramatically. Students won battles but lost the war when government moved multiple bad policies simultaneously.

trigger: September 2019: student protests at parliament. Police responded with tear gas and rubber bullets; at least 2 killed. Simultaneously, government attempted to pass multiple bills weakening various laws.
outcome: Some bills withdrawn. But KPK revisions passed; new supervisory board effectively hobbled the body. KPK conviction rate dropped dramatically after 2020. Students won some battles, lost the war. Indonesia's anti-corruption gains were significantly reversed.
intervention_levers: Student coordination across universities, anti-corruption coalition building, focusing on single issue (avoiding diffusion), international transparency organization pressure, electoral consequences for supporting legislators.

name: Bangladesh Garment Workers — Rana Plaza
issue: Fatal conditions in garment supply chains; corporate accountability for worker deaths in Global South factories.

ncrs.rationale: 1,134 deaths in single collapse created undeniable trigger. Brand labels in rubble made supply chain visible. Bangladesh Accord signed by 200+ brands. One million workers covered by improved safety inspections. Model for supply chain accountability campaigns.

trigger: April 24, 2013: Rana Plaza — an 8-story building housing 5 garment factories — collapsed, killing 1,134 workers. Workers had been ordered in despite visible cracks the day before. Building had been built illegally with substandard materials.
outcome: Bangladesh Accord on Fire and Building Safety signed by 200+ brands (legally binding). Better-than-previous garment worker safety inspections. One million garment workers covered. Owner Sohel Rana arrested. But: Bangladesh Accord initially had 5-year lifespan; successor agreements cover fewer factories.
intervention_levers: Brand-specific campaigns, legally binding accord structure, anniversary commemoration, Fashion Revolution Day, supply chain transparency legislation advocacy, worker organizing support.

name: Fridays for Future — Global South Expansion
issue: Climate change as existential threat disproportionately affecting Global South nations that contributed least to emissions.

ncrs.rationale: Cropping of Vanessa Nakate from AP photo became powerful advocacy for centering Global South voices. MAPA framing entered mainstream discourse. Loss and damage language emerged. Movement successfully diversified climate activism's public face.

trigger: The cropping of Vanessa Nakate out of an AP wire photo in January 2020 (while four white European climate activists remained) created a global media storm about climate movement's racial politics and the erasure of African climate voices.
outcome: Global South activists became central to FFF's public identity, not peripheral. Nakate wrote "A Bigger Picture" (2021). UNFCCC negotiations began more meaningfully including MAPA (Most Affected People and Areas) framing. Climate loss-and-damage language entered mainstream discourse.
intervention_levers: Centering MAPA voices, challenging media representation, connecting climate to development justice, loss and damage advocacy, regional coordination, book and documentary production.
slogans: You're never too small to make a difference

name: Bolivia Water Wars
issue: Privatization of Cochabamba's water supply to Bechtel Corporation; price increases of 200-300%.

ncrs.rationale: Complete victory — privatization reversed, Bechtel expelled. Established "water is a human right" as global campaign frame. Influenced UN's 2010 recognition. Model for Bolivia's leftward political turn; Evo Morales elected 2005.

trigger: January 2000: First protest when water bills arrived showing 200-300% increases. Escalating mobilizations through February-March. April 2000: Coordinadora de Defensa del Agua y de la Vida called general strike.
outcome: April 10, 2000: Government reversed privatization — unprecedented. Bechtel expelled. Coordinator leader Oscar Olivera became global symbol. Case studied internationally; influenced UN's 2010 recognition of water as a human right. Model for Bolivia's broader leftward political turn; Evo Morales elected 2005.
intervention_levers: Coalition-building (factory workers, farmers, professionals), general strike, international solidarity, legal challenges to privatization contracts, "water as human right" framing, connecting to broader anti-privatization movement.
slogans: El Agua Es Nuestra (The Water Is Ours)

name: Ecuador Rights of Nature / Pacha Mama Constitution
issue: Constitutional recognition of nature's inherent rights (Rights of Nature / Derechos de la Naturaleza).

ncrs.rationale: World's first constitution granting Nature legal rights. Bolivia followed (2009). Multiple US cities adopted similar ordinances. Enforcement weak under same government that passed it, but created legal category that will enable future campaigns globally.

trigger: Constituent Assembly process 2007-2008 became the vehicle — civic organizations successfully lobbied to include Rights of Nature articles. The assembly process itself was the trigger mechanism.
outcome: World's first constitution granting Nature legal rights (Articles 71-74). "Nature has the right to integral respect for its existence and for the maintenance and regeneration of its life cycles." Used in subsequent litigation. Bolivia followed (2009). Multiple US cities adopted similar ordinances. However, enforcement under Correa government was weak.
intervention_levers: Constitutional assembly lobbying, indigenous rights framing, international legal expertise, connecting to Buen Vivir philosophy, litigation using new rights, replication in other jurisdictions.
slogans: Derechos de la Naturaleza (Rights of Nature)

name: Mexico: Ayotzinapa 43
issue: Forced disappearance of 43 teacher-training students in Iguala, Guerrero by police in collusion with drug cartel.

ncrs.rationale: 43 specific names and faces did what statistics about 60,000 disappearances couldn't. Monthly marches continue 10+ years later. Peña Nieto government permanently discredited. López Obrador elected partly on anti-impunity platform. However, most students still not found.

trigger: Night of September 26-27, 2014: Police in Iguala (on orders connected to mayor's wife's political event) attacked the students' buses. 43 students disappeared. Six killed. Government's "historical truth" rejected by independent expert group.
outcome: Government's "historical truth" rejected by independent expert group (GIEI). Ayotzinapa case became the defining symbol of Mexico's impunity crisis. Peña Nieto's government permanently discredited. López Obrador won 2018 election partly on anti-impunity platform. But: as of 2025, most students still not found.
intervention_levers: Monthly marches, international expert group (GIEI), documentary filmmaking, artistic commemoration, electoral consequences, connecting to broader impunity crisis, sustained family leadership.
slogans: Vivos Se Los Llevaron, Vivos Los Queremos

name: Guatemala Anti-Corruption — "CICIG Protests"
issue: Presidential corruption exposed by CICIG (UN anti-corruption commission); demand for President Molina's resignation.

ncrs.rationale: CICIG's institutional validation was crucial — UN body with evidence, not just activist allegations. President resigned two days before election. However, US (under Trump) subsequently pressured to end CICIG. Pattern shows institutional validators can be withdrawn.

trigger: April 16, 2015: CICIG's press conference revealing the customs fraud scheme and linking it directly to VP Baldetti (arrested May 2015) and subsequently President Otto Pérez Molina.
outcome: Pérez Molina resigned September 3, 2015 — two days before the presidential election. Unprecedented. CICIG's existence as external institutional validator was crucial — not just an NGO making allegations but a UN body with evidence. Pattern repeated: US subsequently pressured (under Trump) to end CICIG.
intervention_levers: UN institutional validation, investigative capacity, middle-class mobilization, social media coordination, electoral timing pressure, international diplomatic support, protecting CICIG mandate.

name: Peru Mass Protests — Boluarte Government
issue: Pedro Castillo's removal as president and Dina Boluarte's assumption of power; indigenous communities demand new elections.

ncrs.rationale: Protests sustained for months but neither demand (elections, Boluarte resignation) met. 60+ killed by security forces. Structural pattern — Lima elites vs. Andean communities — unchanged. Movement correctly identified colonial divide but lacked institutional leverage.

trigger: December 7, 2022: Castillo attempted to dissolve Congress and rule by emergency decree. Congress removed him. Boluarte (his VP, immediately realigned with Lima elites) took over. Military deployed; at least 60 killed in subsequent protests.
outcome: Protests demanding immediate elections, Boluarte resignation. Neither demand met. Protests sustained for months but gradually lost momentum. Boluarte remained in office. Trial court proceedings against Castillo ongoing. Structural pattern — Lima elites vs. Andean communities — remains unchanged.
intervention_levers: Indigenous organization coordination, international human rights pressure, documentation of police violence, connecting to regional solidarity networks, constitutional reform advocacy.

name: Honduras: Berta Cáceres / COPINH
issue: Agua Zarca dam threatening Lenca indigenous territory and the Gualcarque River (sacred).

ncrs.rationale: Goldman Prize made Berta globally visible — her murder became international story. Dam canceled, company executives convicted. However, Berta is dead and Honduras remains world's most dangerous country for environmental defenders.

trigger: March 3, 2016: Berta Cáceres was assassinated in her home. Seven bullets. The assassination of a Goldman Prize winner — with maximum international visibility — created a global shock.
outcome: Agua Zarca dam canceled November 2016. German company Voith Hydro withdrew. Financiers withdrew. International investors paid attention globally. Desa (dam company) executives convicted for Cáceres' murder 2021. The dam was stopped. But Berta is dead, and Honduras continues to be the world's most dangerous country for environmental defenders.
intervention_levers: Goldman Prize visibility, international financier pressure, murder investigation and prosecution, documentary filmmaking, COPINH organizational continuity, connecting to broader environmental defender protection.

name: Haiti: Anti-Corruption / PetroCaribe
issue: $4.2 billion PetroCaribe oil fund money diverted by elites; government corruption and impunity.

ncrs.rationale: Movement correctly identified corruption but couldn't build institutional alternatives. President Moïse assassinated 2021. Haiti descended into gang-controlled chaos. Exposing corruption without building alternatives accelerated collapse.

trigger: July 2018: government raised fuel prices 51% overnight (reducing subsidies under IMF pressure). Violent protests immediately. October 2019: second wave triggered by Senate commission report confirming $4.2 billion disappeared.
outcome: President Moïse survived. Prime Ministers changed multiple times. Moïse assassinated July 2021 (unrelated to these protests but in context of total state failure). Haiti descended into gang-controlled chaos by 2022-2024. The movement identified the corruption correctly but could not build institutional alternatives.
intervention_levers: Diaspora coordination, accountability journalism, international asset tracing, connecting to broader state-building efforts, UN peacekeeping engagement, supporting civil society capacity.
slogans: Kot Kòb PetroCaribe a? (Where is the PetroCaribe money?)

name: Brazil: Amazon Defenders / Yanomami Crisis
issue: Bolsonaro's dismantling of environmental enforcement; illegal mining on indigenous territories and starvation of Yanomami.

ncrs.rationale: Government suppression of documentation meant visual evidence created maximum moral shock when it emerged under Lula. Deforestation decreased 50% in first year of new government. However, illegal mining ongoing; structural extractive economy unchanged.

trigger: January 2023: Lula took office; inspection of Yanomami territory revealed starvation, malnutrition, malaria in children that had gone undocumented for 4 years while Bolsonaro suppressed reporting. Images of starving children shocked nation.
outcome: Lula declared Yanomami humanitarian crisis. Military deployed to expel illegal miners ("garimpeiros"). Amazon deforestation decreased 50% in first year of Lula government. Bruno Pereira's killers convicted. However: illegal mining ongoing; garimpeiro expulsion incomplete; structural extractive economy unchanged.
intervention_levers: Indigenous organization coordination, international media attention, satellite monitoring, electoral consequences, new government enforcement capacity, connecting to global climate framework.

name: Tunisia Arab Spring
issue: Ben Ali's 23-year authoritarian rule, unemployment, police brutality, corruption.

ncrs.rationale: Only Arab Spring country to achieve genuine democratic transition. Ben Ali fled in 28 days. National Dialogue Quartet won Nobel Peace Prize. However, 2019 Saied election and subsequent authoritarian consolidation shows fragility of democratic gains.

trigger: December 17, 2010: Mohamed Bouazizi, 26-year-old street vendor in Sidi Bouzid, set himself on fire after police confiscated his vegetable cart and (allegedly) slapped him. A protest form that people immediately understood as dignity, not despair.
outcome: Ben Ali fled to Saudi Arabia January 14, 2011 — 28 days after Bouazizi's self-immolation. The only Arab Spring country that subsequently achieved genuine democratic transition (2014 Nobel Peace Prize for National Dialogue Quartet). 2019: President Saied elected on anti-corruption platform, then conducted autocratic consolidation.
intervention_levers: Labor union infrastructure (UGTT), civil society coordination, constitutional reform process, international democratic support, National Dialogue mechanism, protecting independent media.
slogans: الشعب يريد إسقاط النظام (The people want to bring down the regime)

name: Egypt Arab Spring
issue: Mubarak's 30-year rule, corruption, police brutality, economic stagnation — and the failure to consolidate democratic transition.

ncrs.rationale: Won the battle (Mubarak fell in 18 days) but lost the war. Morsi elected then deposed. Sisi's military coup and Rabaa Massacre. Egypt now more authoritarian than under Mubarak. Visual victory obscured absence of institutional safeguards.

trigger: January 25, 2011 "Police Day": Day of Rage called following Tunisia's success. Tahrir Square occupation began. 18 days of massive protests.
outcome: Mubarak resigned February 11, 2011. Muslim Brotherhood's Mohamed Morsi elected June 2012 — first democratic election. Morsi's authoritarian moves, constitutional crisis. Military coup July 3, 2013. Abdel Fattah el-Sisi seized power. Mass killings of Brotherhood supporters (Rabaa Massacre). Egypt now more authoritarian than under Mubarak.
intervention_levers: Limited — the institutional vacuum was filled by military. Future interventions would need to focus on economic pressure, international human rights mechanisms, supporting independent civil society and media in exile, prisoner advocacy.
slogans: The people demand the fall of the regime

name: Iran Green Movement
issue: Disputed re-election of Ahmadinejad; demands for democratic reform and honest elections.

ncrs.rationale: Neda Agha-Soltan's death became globally iconic. However, movement suppressed by September 2009. Leaders under house arrest for 14+ years. Visual power without material power to protect movement ultimately couldn't change outcomes.

trigger: June 12, 2009: Official election results announced with Ahmadinejad winning by implausible margin. Supporters of Mousavi and Karroubi immediately disputed results. Millions in streets within days.
outcome: Movement suppressed by June-September 2009. Leaders Mousavi and Karroubi placed under house arrest (2011); Karroubi still under house arrest as of 2025. No electoral reform. Movement's networks maintained underground; reemerged in 2019 and 2022 protests. Permission structure in Iran deeply hostile to any democratic movement.
intervention_levers: International human rights pressure, diaspora coordination, technology access (circumvention tools), documenting repression, sanctions targeting specific human rights violators, supporting underground networks.

name: Iran: Mahsa Amini Protests
issue: Compulsory hijab enforcement and the death of Mahsa (Zhina) Amini in morality police custody.

ncrs.rationale: "Woman, Life, Freedom" became globally resonant. Protests lasted 6+ months despite 500+ killed, 15,000+ arrested. Morality police temporarily suspended. No systemic change but movement broke fear barrier more completely than 2009.

trigger: Mahsa Amini's death. Young women publicly removing and burning hijabs. "Woman, Life, Freedom" (Jin, Jiyan, Azadî — in Kurdish) became the chant and global slogan.
outcome: Protests lasted 6+ months. 500+ killed (estimates vary widely). 15,000+ arrested. Several executions directly connected to protests. No systemic political change. Morality police temporarily suspended (later reinstated in new form). New hijab legislation passed 2023 with harsher penalties. The movement broke the fear barrier more completely than any previous Iranian protest.
intervention_levers: Diaspora amplification, technology access for documentation, international sanctions on specific officials, celebrity amplification, connecting to global feminist solidarity, supporting underground networks.

name: Lebanon Thawra
issue: Economic collapse, political corruption across all sectarian factions, WhatsApp tax as final insult.

ncrs.rationale: "All of them means all of them" was perfect accountability framing. PM resigned within 12 days. However, no structural change. Then COVID. Then Beirut port explosion. Economic collapse continued — 90% poverty rate by 2021. Movement identified problem perfectly but couldn't fill vacuum.

trigger: October 17, 2019: Government announced $0.20/day tax on WhatsApp calls — a direct charge on the free communication Lebanese used to avoid expensive telecom monopolies. The absurdity of the specific tax crystallized decades of elite extraction.
outcome: PM Hariri resigned October 29, 2019. But: no structural political change. Then COVID. Then August 4, 2020 Beirut port explosion — 218 killed, 7,000 wounded, 300,000 homeless. Economic collapse continued; by 2021 Lebanon had 90% poverty rate. The revolution identified the problem perfectly but couldn't build institutional alternatives.
intervention_levers: Cross-sectarian coalition building, international investigation of port explosion, diaspora coordination, supporting independent candidates in elections, humanitarian aid, accountability journalism.
slogans: Kellon yaani kellon (All of them means all)

name: Israel Judicial Reform Protests
issue: Netanyahu government's attempted judicial overhaul eliminating Supreme Court's ability to override government decisions.

ncrs.rationale: Military reserve pilots' defection letter created immediate crisis — security elite withdrawal made abstract debate material. First element passed July 2023. October 7 attacks suspended protests. Judicial reform paused but not abandoned.

trigger: Israeli Military Reserve Pilot letter announcing refusal to report for duty if judicial overhaul passed (March-April 2023). The security elite's defection created a crisis — Israel's military deterrent depends on reserve volunteers.
outcome: July 2023: Knesset passed first element of overhaul (eliminating "reasonableness" standard). October 7, 2023: Hamas attacks fundamentally changed context — protests suspended during war. As of 2025: judicial reform effectively paused but not abandoned; Netanyahu government continued under emergency conditions.
intervention_levers: Military reserve leverage, weekly protest infrastructure, business community pressure, diaspora coordination, connecting to broader democratic values, high-tech sector economic pressure.

name: Jordan Bread Protests
issue: IMF-mandated austerity measures including bread price increases and income tax reform.

ncrs.rationale: PM resigned within 4 days. Austerity measures revised. Professional unions' involvement signaled middle class pain. Limited structural change but demonstrated Jordanian regime vulnerability to cross-class mobilization.

trigger: June 1, 2018: Midnight fuel price increase (following subsidy removal) triggered spontaneous protests. Then bread prices rise. General strike June 4.
outcome: PM Hani Mulki resigned June 4. Austerity measures revised. Limited structural change; IMF conditions remained. But demonstrated that Jordanian regime was not immune to popular pressure when it reached across class lines.
intervention_levers: Professional union coordination, general strike capacity, connecting to broader Arab Spring legacy, regional diplomatic pressure, IMF policy engagement.

name: Morocco: 2016 Youth Boycott Campaign
issue: Price gouging by Centrale Laitière (milk), Afriquia (gas stations), and Marjane (supermarkets) — all owned by political elites.

ncrs.rationale: Danone revenues dropped 40-50% within a week. Prices reduced. Demonstrated economic leverage of coordinated consumer action. Three specific companies, specific products, specific owners made boycott maximally replicable.

trigger: April 2018: Boycott campaign launched via WhatsApp and social media calling for boycott of three specific companies. The campaign spread with extraordinary speed — within a week, millions were participating.
outcome: Danone's Morocco revenues dropped 40-50%; company was forced to cut prices. Gas prices reduced. Campaign demonstrated economic leverage of coordinated consumer action. The PM Akhannouch went on to win 2021 elections — movement's economic impact exceeded its political impact. But boycott lasted months.
intervention_levers: WhatsApp coordination, consumer boycott as participation mechanism, specific corporate targets with visible ownership, social media amplification, connecting to broader inequality narrative.

name: Yellow Vests France
issue: Fuel tax increase as economic injustice for rural and suburban French workers; broader inequality.

ncrs.rationale: Won immediate concessions (fuel tax suspended, €10B in relief). However, no lasting organization built. Movement infiltrated by far right. Macron survived. Leaderless movement forced concessions but couldn't consolidate them.

trigger: November 17, 2018: 300,000 "Yellow Vests" (fluorescent safety vests, mandatory in all French cars — genius: everyone already owned one) blockaded roundabouts and roads across France. November 24: violence in Paris.
outcome: Fuel tax suspended. Grand Débat National (national consultation) launched. €10 billion in immediate concessions (minimum wage increase, tax relief). Tactical wins. But: no structural change to inequality. Movement eventually infiltrated by far right. No lasting organization built. Macron survived. Long-term impact: Le Pen strengthened.
intervention_levers: Decentralized coordination, roundabout blockade as tactic, connecting to broader inequality narrative, attempting to build lasting organization, resisting far-right infiltration.
slogans: On lâche rien (We won't give up)

name: Occupy Wall Street
issue: Economic inequality, Wall Street impunity after 2008 financial crisis, corporate capture of democracy.

ncrs.rationale: "We are the 99%" permanently entered political vocabulary. No policy wins. But inequality framing shaped Bernie Sanders campaigns and subsequent progressive politics. Movement built vocabulary; campaigns convert vocabulary into change.

trigger: September 17, 2011: Occupation began. Low trigger potency — the occupation was the announcement, not a response to a specific injustice. "We are the 99%" became the dominant frame (Kalle Lasn, Adbusters).
outcome: Movements cleared by coordinated municipal actions November-December 2011. No policy wins. But: "we are the 99%" and inequality framing permanently entered political vocabulary. Bernie Sanders 2016/2020 campaigns drew directly from Occupy vocabulary. The cultural impact exceeded the movement's operational wins.
intervention_levers: Occupation as tactic, framing and messaging, connecting to electoral politics (later), building lasting organizations (later — DSA growth), labor movement connections.
slogans: Banks got bailed out, we got sold out

name: Polish Women's Strike
issue: Constitutional Tribunal ruling effectively banning abortion even for fetal abnormalities; reproductive rights.

ncrs.rationale: Red lightning bolt became powerful protest symbol. Largest protests in Poland since 1989. Ruling went into effect but delayed implementation. PiS lost 2023 elections — abortion was #1 issue for women under 40. Electoral consequences demonstrated.

trigger: October 22, 2020: Constitutional Tribunal (packed with PiS nominees) ruled that fetal abnormality is not sufficient justification for abortion — the one exception most commonly used. The ruling was immediately understood as near-total abortion ban.
outcome: Ruling technically went into effect January 2021. Government was forced to delay implementation. PiS lost 2023 elections partially due to women's vote — exit polls showed abortion was the #1 issue for women under 40. New coalition government (Tusk) attempting to liberalize abortion despite Presidential veto.
intervention_levers: Strike as tactic, red lightning bolt symbol, church disruption, international solidarity, electoral consequences, connecting to broader women's rights framework, judicial reform advocacy.

name: Iceland: Banking Revolution / Pots and Pans
issue: Banking collapse; holding bankers criminally accountable; new constitution.

ncrs.rationale: 26 senior bankers imprisoned — highest per capita in world. Economy recovered faster than countries that accepted austerity. Global model for 'let banks fail, prosecute bankers.' Small country size enabled rapid mobilization and accountability.

trigger: October 2008 bank collapse + January 2009 Saturday protests at parliament (Alþingi) — citizens banged pots and pans (old protest tradition) outside parliament. Prime Minister Haarde became the first world leader convicted of criminal negligence related to 2008 crisis.
outcome: Bankers prosecuted (26 senior bankers imprisoned, highest per capita in world). New constitution crowdsourced (2012, though ultimately not adopted in full). Economy recovered faster than Ireland, Greece, Portugal (who accepted austerity). Became global model for "let banks fail, prosecute bankers."
intervention_levers: Pots and pans tradition, Saturday protest format, prosecutorial independence, crowdsourced constitution process, international media attention, small country rapid mobilization.

name: Spain Indignados / 15-M
issue: Youth unemployment (50%), austerity cuts, corruption in PP and PSOE parties; demand for "real democracy."

ncrs.rationale: No immediate policy wins. But Podemos formed directly from 15-M networks and entered government 2020. Municipal activists won city halls in Madrid and Barcelona. Movement was seedbed for political party formation — long-term success despite short-term failure.

trigger: May 15, 2011 (hence "15-M"): 130 cities; tens of thousands. Madrid's Puerta del Sol occupied. A small march became an occupation when 40 people decided to stay the night; next morning 20,000 joined.
outcome: No immediate policy wins. But: Podemos party formed 2014 directly from Indignados networks. Podemos subsequently entered government 2020. 15-M transformed Spanish political landscape; two-party system disrupted permanently. Municipal activists ("Mayors of Change") won city halls in Madrid and Barcelona.
intervention_levers: Plaza occupation model, assembly-based decision making, connecting to electoral politics, party formation, municipal campaigns, worker cooperatives, housing movement.

name: Urgenda Climate Victory — Netherlands
issue: Dutch government insufficiently ambitious climate targets; failure to protect citizens from climate risk.

ncrs.rationale: World's first successful climate liability case against a government. Supreme Court upheld — government legally required to cut emissions. Blueprint traveled globally, spawning climate litigation movement. Legal pathway more effective than street protest in this context.

trigger: June 24, 2015: District Court ruled in Urgenda's favor — government must cut emissions 25% by 2020. The ruling itself was the trigger. Government appealed; October 2018 Court of Appeal upheld. December 2019: Supreme Court upheld — final victory.
outcome: Netherlands bound by court order to cut emissions 25% by 2020. Policy changes implemented. But: Dutch government has still not fully complied; enforcement mechanisms limited. More important: the legal pathway was established globally. Climate litigation became a major movement strategy after 2015.
intervention_levers: Strategic litigation, human rights framing, scientific evidence, government accountability, replication in other jurisdictions, connecting to broader climate movement, enforcement monitoring.

name: Germany Constitutional Climate Ruling
issue: German Climate Action Programme's insufficient emissions reduction targets for post-2030 period.

ncrs.rationale: First constitutional court to rule climate targets unconstitutional based on rights of future generations. Germany revised law within weeks — net zero by 2045. Rights-of-future-generations framework now influencing other constitutional interpretations globally.

trigger: April 29, 2021: Federal Constitutional Court ruled that Germany's Climate Protection Act was unconstitutional — insufficient targets violated the rights of future generations. First constitutional court to establish this principle.
outcome: Germany revised Climate Protection Act within weeks — accelerated targets. "Net zero by 2045" target established. More significant: the rights-of-future-generations framework was established in German jurisprudence and began influencing other countries' constitutional interpretation.
intervention_levers: Constitutional litigation, rights-of-future-generations framing, youth plaintiffs, scientific evidence, rapid legislative response pressure, international precedent amplification.

name: Anti-Austerity Greece
issue: IMF/EU austerity memoranda destroying social protections; democratic sovereignty vs. creditor demands.

ncrs.rationale: 61.3% voted 'No' to austerity in referendum — clearest democratic expression. Tsipras accepted third memorandum anyway within days. Economic coercion (ECB threat) overrode democratic decision. Movement's democratic win was overridden by institutional power.

trigger: January 25, 2015: SYRIZA won parliamentary elections on explicit anti-austerity platform. Tsipras became PM. July 2015 referendum: Greek people voted 61.3% AGAINST further austerity — clearest democratic expression of anti-austerity sentiment anywhere.
outcome: Despite 61.3% "No" vote, Tsipras accepted third memorandum anyway — July 13, 2015. Third bailout was harsher than what voters had rejected. Economic coercion (threat of bank closures, Grexit) overrode democratic decision. SYRIZA subsequently governed as austerity implementer. Movement's democratic win was meaningless against Eurozone institutional power.
intervention_levers: Limited when facing Eurozone institutional power. Possible: building European-wide anti-austerity coalition, Grexit preparation as leverage, international solidarity, alternative economic models.
slogans: We are not paying for their crisis

name: Extinction Rebellion — UK Launch
issue: Climate emergency; government failure to act; demand for climate citizens' assembly.

ncrs.rationale: UK Parliament declared 'climate emergency' (symbolic). Net Zero 2050 target set (less ambitious than demanded). XR's three demands generated no binding policy. Visual spectacle without winnable demands is common failure mode. COVID ended street protest model.

trigger: April 2019: International Rebellion — simultaneous actions in 33 countries, London shut down for 11 days. Waterloo Bridge occupation with garden. Dramatic civil disobedience including blocking the London Stock Exchange.
outcome: UK Parliament declared "climate emergency" (largely symbolic). Net Zero by 2050 target (less ambitious than scientists demanded) set. But XR's three demands (Tell the Truth, Act Now, Citizens' Assembly) generated no binding policy. 2020: COVID ended street protest model. Internal tensions over tactics and demands.
intervention_levers: Civil disobedience normalization, arrest as tactic, international coordination, citizens' assembly demand, artistic expression, but needs tighter connection between disruption and specific winnable demands.

name: Montreal Protocol — Ozone Layer
issue: Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) destroying the stratospheric ozone layer; UV radiation threat to human health.

ncrs.rationale: Most successful environmental treaty in history. CFCs phased out globally. Ozone layer recovering (projected healed by 2065). Only case of humanity reversing planetary-scale crisis. Key factors: clear harm-solution link, available substitutes, industry cooperation.

trigger: 1985: "Ozone hole" confirmed; simultaneous: skin cancer connection made visceral and personal. The UV-B = skin cancer narrative made the abstract ozone science personally threatening to anyone who went outside.
outcome: Montreal Protocol 1987 — the most successful environmental treaty in history. CFCs phased out globally. The ozone layer is measurably recovering (projected fully healed by 2065). The only case of humanity successfully reversing a planetary-scale environmental crisis once underway. Key factors: the harm-solution link was clear, substitutes were available, and industry cooperation was secured.
intervention_levers: Scientific consensus communication, skin cancer framing, substitute technology development, industry negotiation, international treaty framework, enforcement mechanisms, progressive strengthening.

name: Ottawa Treaty — Landmines
issue: Anti-personnel landmines killing and maiming civilians long after conflicts ended.

ncrs.rationale: 164 state parties. Production by signatories effectively eliminated. Casualties dropped from 26,000/year to ~7,000/year. Princess Diana's images connected mass audience to obscure crisis. Nobel Prize validated approach. Not universal (major powers not signatories) but established treaty-path model.

trigger: The combination: (1) Princess Diana's photos in minefield/with landmine victims, December 1996-January 1997; (2) Canadian government hosting the final Ottawa conference as forcing function; (3) Jody Williams and ICBL sustained organizing over years.
outcome: Ottawa Treaty signed 1997; 164 state parties as of 2024. Landmine production and use by signatory states effectively eliminated. Annual civilian casualties from landmines dropped from 26,000/year (1997) to ~7,000/year (2022). Not universal (US, Russia, China, India not signatories). But: established a model for treaty-path campaigns on weapons (cluster munitions followed).
intervention_levers: Celebrity amplification (Princess Diana), NGO coalition (ICBL), middle-power government leadership (Canada), survivor testimony, medical community engagement, clear ask (ban), economic data on clearance costs.

name: Paris Agreement
issue: Internationally binding framework for limiting global temperature rise to 1.5-2°C above pre-industrial levels.

ncrs.rationale: 196 parties signed — first universal climate agreement. However, NDCs not legally binding; world still on 2.7°C trajectory. Agreement chose universality over ambition. Political win came at cost of enforcement mechanisms.

trigger: Pre-negotiation: Typhoon Hainan (Philippines, 2013) — Yeb Saño's tearful speech at Warsaw COP created global moral pressure. The September 2014 Climate March (400,000 in NYC) demonstrated public will. French diplomatic mastery at COP21.
outcome: 196 parties signed. First universal climate agreement. However: NDCs are not legally binding; aggregate targets still put world on 2.7°C trajectory; US withdrew under Trump; rejoined under Biden. The agreement's legal design chose universality over ambition — the political win came at the cost of enforcement mechanisms.
intervention_levers: Climate marches, COP timing, diplomatic coalition-building, business engagement, scientific consensus communication, connecting to extreme weather events, subsequent litigation using Paris as framework.

name: Plastics Treaty Negotiations
issue: Global binding treaty to end plastic pollution, including production caps.

ncrs.rationale: INC-5 collapsed without agreement. Petrostate bloc (Saudi, Russia, Iran) blocked production caps. Industry lobby effective. General awareness campaigns succeeded but didn't create domestic pressure on blocking states. Treaty in permission-building phase, not ready to tip.

trigger: INC-5 in Busan (November-December 2024): negotiations collapsed without agreement. Petrostate bloc (Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iran, US under Trump) blocked production caps. Industry lobby (American Chemistry Council) well-organized.
outcome: No treaty. INC-6 tentatively planned but timeline uncertain. Plastic production set to triple by 2050 under current trajectory. The treaty campaign is not dead — it is in the stall pattern.
intervention_levers: Targeting petrostate bloc domestic audiences, corporate campaigns against plastic producers, brand accountability, connecting to health impacts, building High Ambition Coalition support, alternative materials development.

name: COVAX — Vaccine Equity
issue: Equitable global distribution of COVID-19 vaccines; preventing vaccine nationalism by wealthy countries.

ncrs.rationale: COVAX delivered less than half its target. Vaccine nationalism prevailed. Omicron emerged in unvaccinated populations. Good institutional design insufficient when economic incentives pointed away. Campaign needed to target IP waivers, not the failing institution.

trigger: No single positive trigger — rather a cascade of failures: rich countries (UK, US, EU) hoarded vaccines beyond need while COVAX received shortfalls. India, major COVAX supplier through Serum Institute, restricted exports during its COVID wave.
outcome: COVAX delivered 1.9 billion doses — less than half its 2021 target; reached fewer than promised number of countries by 2021 deadline. Vaccine nationalism prevailed. Omicron variant emerged in un-vaccinated populations (South Africa) — demonstrating the strategic idiocy of vaccine inequity from a pure public health perspective.
intervention_levers: TRIPS waiver advocacy, compulsory licensing, pharmaceutical company pressure, bilateral donation diplomacy, manufacturing capacity building, connecting equity to variant emergence narrative.
slogans: No one is safe until everyone is safe

name: Arab Spring — Syria
issue: Assad regime's repression, corruption, drought-driven displacement — initially peaceful democracy demands.

ncrs.rationale: 500,000+ dead. 12 million displaced. Chemical weapons used. Assad survived until December 2024 HTS overthrow. No democratic movement — civil war. Demonstrates that trigger can ignite catastrophe as easily as revolution when organizational infrastructure and protection are absent.

trigger: March 2011: Teenage boys in Daraa spray-painted anti-regime graffiti. Their arrest and torture by security forces triggered local protests. Assad's violent response triggered nationwide mobilization.
outcome: Civil war. 500,000+ dead. 12 million displaced (half the prewar population). Chemical weapons used. Assad regime survived (until December 2024 when HTS overthrew Assad following military collapse). Intervention by Russia, Iran, Hezbollah, and then US/Turkey fundamentally changed conflict dynamics. No democratic movement achieved its goals.
intervention_levers: Limited — once civil war begins, nonviolent movement tools are inadequate. Humanitarian protection, refugee support, documentation for accountability, post-conflict justice planning.

name: West Papua Independence Movement
issue: Papuan indigenous people's right to self-determination; occupation by Indonesia since 1969 "Act of Free Choice."

ncrs.rationale: Morally compelling but geopolitically invisible. No major state willing to apply pressure on Indonesia. Armed resistance militarily weak. Visual documentation is the missing ingredient — catastrophe is real but politically invisible.

trigger: Multiple triggers have failed to produce international tipping: 2019 protests in Wamena following racist slurs against Papuan students; multiple documentation of military operations. Each trigger generates local response but no sustained international pressure.
outcome: Movement has not produced self-determination. Armed resistance (OPM) ongoing but militarily weak. Some international solidarity. Papuan leaders continue to be arrested and imprisoned. The corporate extraction (Freeport) continues largely unimpeded. The most persistent "invisible movement" in this database.
intervention_levers: International media attention, corporate campaigns against Freeport, regional diplomatic pressure, UN human rights mechanisms, diaspora organizing, visual documentation of military operations.

name: HIV/AIDS: ACT UP Treatment Access
