issue: Government inaction on AIDS epidemic; FDA drug approval delays killing gay men and others; pharma pricing.

ncrs.rationale: FDA approval accelerated from 10+ years to under 2 years. HIV became manageable with combination therapy. 'Silence = Death' is among most effective activist visual campaigns in history. Life-or-death urgency drove relentless leadership.

trigger: ACT UP (AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power) founded March 1987 after Larry Kramer's speech at Lesbian and Gay Community Services Center, NYC. Target: FDA's drug approval process taking 10+ years while people died.
outcome: FDA approval process accelerated from 10+ years to under 2 years. Combination antiretroviral therapy approved 1996; HIV became manageable. AZT price forced down through activism and international pressure. Created global AIDS activist infrastructure that subsequently fought for generic drug access globally.
intervention_levers: Direct action at FDA, 'Silence = Death' visual campaign, die-ins, pharmaceutical company targeting, connecting to global generic access, leveraging medical expertise within movement.

name: South Africa: Treatment Action Campaign / AIDS Treatment
issue: Thabo Mbeki's AIDS denialism and pharmaceutical companies' patent protection preventing generic ARV access for 5 million+ HIV-positive South Africans.

ncrs.rationale: 39 pharmaceutical companies abandoned South Africa lawsuit. Government began world's largest ARV program. ARV prices reduced 99% globally. Zackie Achmat's personal sacrifice created moral symbol no campaign image could manufacture.

trigger: 2001: 39 pharmaceutical companies abandoned the South Africa lawsuit under international pressure and TAC's campaign — global public outrage at companies suing a developing country to protect drug profits while millions died.
outcome: Government began ARV rollout 2004 — became world's largest ARV treatment program. Mbeki's denialism eventually politically isolated. ARV prices globally reduced 99% over subsequent decade. The TAC case changed the global framework for pharmaceutical patents and public health (Doha Declaration still used).
intervention_levers: Constitutional litigation, international pharma pressure, Doha Declaration advocacy, leader's personal sacrifice, connecting to global access movement, medical community engagement.

name: Jubilee 2000 — Debt Cancellation
issue: Unpayable debt burden on the world's poorest countries — much accumulated through unjust lending to dictators.

ncrs.rationale: G7 Cologne Initiative: $100 billion debt relief for 41 countries. Biblical Jubilee frame did intellectual justification work, freeing slogan to be purely emotional. 'Drop the debt' was most successful simplification of complex macroeconomic issue.

trigger: G8 Birmingham Summit, May 1998: 70,000 people formed a human chain around the conference center demanding debt cancellation. Then: G8 Cologne Summit 1999; Jubilee's momentum hard to ignore.
outcome: G7 Cologne Initiative (1999): $100 billion debt relief for 41 countries. HIPC Enhanced Initiative (expanded 2000). Not full cancellation but unprecedented scale. Jubilee South (Global South–led movement) argued it was insufficient and unjust in design. Longer-term: the framework for debt relief advocacy established.
intervention_levers: Human chain visual, celebrity engagement (Bono, Bob Geldof), church networks, G8 summit timing, biblical framing for religious constituencies, connecting to development outcomes.

name: Fossil Fuel Divestment Movement
issue: Institutional investors' financial complicity in climate crisis through fossil fuel holdings; "carbon bubble" risk.

ncrs.rationale: $40+ trillion divested. Major pension funds, universities, sovereign wealth divested. However, actual fossil fuel investment increased globally — divestment shifted ownership without reducing production. Reputational victory, material impact limited.

trigger: No single trigger — rather a steady accumulation. Swarthmore College student campaign (2012) was the first high-profile divestment campaign. Each university divestment decision amplified the next. Norwegian sovereign wealth fund divestment (2019) was a major institutional tipping point.
outcome: $40+ trillion in assets divested by 2022. Major pension funds, universities, sovereign wealth funds divested (NY State, Norwegian sovereign wealth, Harvard, Oxford). However: actual fossil fuel investment simultaneously increased globally — divestment shifted ownership without reducing production. The reputational and financial impact was real but didn't stop production growth.
intervention_levers: Campus organizing, pension fund campaigns, shareholder activism, carbon bubble financial risk framing, connecting to fiduciary duty, each divestment amplifying the next.

name: Bangladesh Climate Litigation / Youth v. Government
issue: Bangladesh government's continued approval of coal power plants despite climate vulnerability; rights of future generations.

ncrs.rationale: Case ongoing. Significance is in precedent-building — each climate case adds to global body of rights-based climate law. Bangladesh's vulnerability is visually obvious; litigation needs specific harm documentation.

trigger: 2023: Youth climate activists filed landmark climate litigation challenging coal plant approvals on constitutional grounds.
outcome: Case ongoing as of 2025. The significance is in the precedent-building — each climate case that proceeds through a national legal system adds to the global body of rights-based climate law.
intervention_levers: Constitutional litigation, documenting specific community displacement, connecting to global climate litigation network, youth plaintiff visibility, scientific evidence.

name: Pacific Islands: Climate Change as Existential Threat
issue: Sea level rise and coral bleaching as existential threat to low-lying Pacific Island nations.

ncrs.rationale: Loss and damage fund established at COP27 — first acknowledgment of wealthy nations' responsibility. Maldives underwater cabinet meeting was masterpiece of environmental visual activism. Small states leveraged moral authority into disproportionate global impact.

trigger: Recurring: submerged cabinet meetings (Maldives cabinet meeting underwater, 2009); Tuvalu's flooding images. The trigger recurs because these nations are actually experiencing what other countries model abstractly.
outcome: Loss and damage fund established at COP27 (2022) — first time wealthy nations acknowledged financial responsibility for climate harm they caused to vulnerable countries. Limited implementation but significant normative shift. No physical protection; islands continue to flood.
intervention_levers: Moral authority of frontline states, creative visual stunts (underwater cabinet), loss and damage advocacy, ICJ advisory opinion, climate litigation, small state coalition diplomacy.
slogans: We are not drowning, we are fighting

name: Global Anti-Apartheid Movement
issue: South African apartheid regime's systematic racial oppression; divestment and sanctions campaigns globally.

ncrs.rationale: Mandela freed 1990. Apartheid dismantled. First democratic elections 1994. One of most successful long-term international solidarity campaigns. Divestment model directly influenced fossil fuel divestment 30 years later.

trigger: State of emergency in South Africa (1985-86) + TV footage of township violence reaching Western audiences. US Congress overriding Reagan's veto to pass Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act (1986) was a political tipping point.
outcome: Mandela freed 1990. Apartheid dismantled through negotiation 1990-1994. First democratic elections April 1994. One of the most successful long-term international solidarity campaigns in history. The divestment model built here directly influenced fossil fuel divestment 30 years later.
intervention_levers: Divestment and sanctions, cultural boycotts, sports boycotts, congressional pressure, corporate campaigns, connecting to US civil rights movement, sustained leadership over decades.

name: Haiti Earthquake Response → Supply Chain Critique
issue: International aid system's failure to rebuild Haiti; NGO industrial complex critique.

ncrs.rationale: Red Cross CEO resigned. Debate about Haiti aid continues. No structural change to international humanitarian system. '6 houses for $500 million' ratio worked because devastating specificity made abstraction concrete.

trigger: The earthquake itself triggered $13 billion in international aid — the largest humanitarian response in history at that point. But: years later, ProPublica and NPR's investigation revealed the Red Cross built only 6 permanent houses with $500 million.
outcome: Red Cross CEO resigned. Debate about Haiti aid continues. No structural change to international humanitarian system. Haiti remains fragile state; 2021 earthquake and subsequent gang violence confirm systemic failure.
intervention_levers: Investigative journalism, accountability metrics, connecting to broader aid effectiveness debate, supporting local organizations over international NGOs, documentation of specific failures.

name: Uganda Anti-LGBTQ Law — International Response
issue: Uganda's Anti-Homosexuality Act 2023 imposing death penalty for "aggravated homosexuality."

ncrs.rationale: Law enforced despite international economic pressure. External campaigns without domestic organizational capacity face 'foreign interference' dismissal. Visual campaigns need to elevate internal voices, not substitute for them.

trigger: March 2023: Parliament passed the Anti-Homosexuality Act with death penalty provisions. President Museveni signed it May 2023 despite international warnings.
outcome: International economic pressure (World Bank, US aid) has not produced repeal as of 2025. The law is being enforced; LGBTQ Ugandans face increased police violence and social persecution. The international campaign demonstrates that external pressure without domestic organizational capacity is insufficient.
intervention_levers: Supporting underground LGBTQ organizations, asylum pathways, World Bank and bilateral aid pressure, documenting human rights violations, connecting to regional African LGBTQ movements.

name: Colombia: Peace Accord Plebiscite
issue: FARC peace agreement following 52-year civil war; referendum defeat despite broad peace support.

ncrs.rationale: Peace deal rejected 50.2% to 49.8% despite widespread peace support. Visual misinformation campaigns defeated real-world majorities. Revised agreement ratified by Congress. FARC demobilized. Demonstrates importance of visual communication battle.

trigger: October 2, 2016 plebiscite: Colombian voters rejected the peace deal 50.2% to 49.8%. Despite widespread support for ending the war.
outcome: Santos won Nobel Peace Prize in October 2016 for the peace effort. Revised agreement ratified by Congress (not plebiscite) December 2016. FARC demobilized. But: significant FARC dissidents returned to arms. Ongoing violence in rural areas. Santos' immediate successor Duque undermined implementation.
intervention_levers: Counter-disinformation, victim community mobilization, international diplomatic support, implementation monitoring, defending peace agreement against successor government rollback.

name: Bangladesh 1971 Genocide Awareness
issue: Recognition of 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War atrocities as genocide; accountability for Pakistani military crimes.

ncrs.rationale: Concert for Bangladesh established 'celebrity witness/amplifier' model used in every subsequent humanitarian crisis. Independence achieved. International genocide recognition still contested — Pakistan doesn't recognize it. Model's limitation (awareness without accountability) also established.

trigger: The Concert for Bangladesh (George Harrison, Ravi Shankar, August 1, 1971) was the first major rock charity concert — predating Live Aid by 14 years. It raised $12 million and brought the Bangladesh crisis to global attention.
outcome: Bangladesh achieved independence (Pakistani surrender December 1971). Concert model became permanent. International recognition of 1971 as genocide still contested — Pakistan does not recognize it. No criminal accountability. The Concert for Bangladesh's lasting impact was on activist communication strategies.
intervention_levers: Celebrity amplification, documentary evidence, international recognition campaigns, tribunal processes, diaspora organizing, connecting to broader human rights framework.

name: India: Right to Information Movement
issue: Citizens' right to access government information; accountability for corruption in public works.

ncrs.rationale: One of world's strongest transparency laws. Millions of RTI applications filed annually. Documented hundreds of thousands of corruption cases. MKSS model made accountability participatory. RTI activists targeted — dozens murdered — demonstrating movement's threat to corrupt systems.

trigger: 2004 elections brought UPA government to power with Communist Party support — the CPI had long supported RTI. The political window opened.
outcome: Right to Information Act passed 2005 — considered one of the strongest transparency laws in the world. Millions of RTI applications filed annually. Documented hundreds of thousands of cases of government corruption and accountability. RTI activists subsequently targeted — dozens murdered or attacked for exposing corruption.
intervention_levers: Jan Sunwai (public hearings), legislative advocacy, connecting to electoral politics, protecting RTI activists, using RTI for specific accountability campaigns, international transparency standards.

name: Congo: Conflict Minerals / Raise Hope for Congo
issue: Eastern Congo's minerals (coltan, tin, tungsten, gold) financing armed groups through supply chains to consumer electronics.

ncrs.rationale: Dodd-Frank Section 1502 required supply chain disclosure. Significant reduction in some conflict mineral sourcing. DRC conflict ongoing. Section 1502 gutted under Trump. Supply chain transparency model established but enforcement weakened.

trigger: 2010: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform Act included Section 1502 requiring publicly traded companies to disclose whether their products contain "conflict minerals" from DRC. The legislative pathway became the trigger.
outcome: Section 1502 implemented — US companies required to audit supply chains. Significant reduction in some conflict mineral sourcing. But: DRC conflict ongoing; multiple armed groups still active; no comprehensive peace. The Dodd-Frank provision (under Trump 2017) effectively gutted for two years. The supply chain transparency model was established but enforcement weakened.
intervention_levers: Legislative advocacy, corporate supply chain campaigns, electronics industry targeting, SEC enforcement, connecting to broader conflict resolution, supporting local peace initiatives.

name: Philippines: Marcos Disinformation vs. Democracy Movement
issue: Return of Marcos family to power through sophisticated social media disinformation erasing Ferdinand Marcos Sr.'s dictatorship crimes.

ncrs.rationale: Marcos Jr. won with 58.7% landslide. Disinformation campaign more effective than grassroots accountability movement. Visual misinformation rewrote historical record. Counter-campaigns must work at level of memory and witness.

trigger: May 2022 presidential election: Bongbong Marcos won with 58.7% — landslide. The disinformation campaign had been more effective than any grassroots accountability movement.
outcome: Marcos Jr. is president. Robredo (the people's movement candidate) lost despite massive "pink revolution" rallies. Disinformation beat democracy. The movement that organized massive rallies and enthusiasm lost to a sophisticated information operation. Case study in how platform architecture and disinformation can defeat grassroots movements.
intervention_levers: Platform accountability, counter-disinformation infrastructure, historical documentation, survivor testimony, connecting to younger generation through accessible formats, diaspora organizing.

name: Turkey Gezi Park Protests
issue: Demolition of Istanbul's Gezi Park for commercial development; broader opposition to Erdoğan's authoritarian consolidation.

ncrs.rationale: Gezi Park not demolished — tactical victory. But Erdoğan used protests to justify crackdowns. Structural democratic decline continued. 2016 coup attempt accelerated authoritarian consolidation. Red dress photograph became iconic through spontaneous documentation.

trigger: May 28, 2013: Bulldozers moved into Gezi Park. Forty environmentalists' sit-in. Police used tear gas and water cannons to clear them. Next day: tens of thousands in the park.
outcome: Gezi Park not demolished. But: Erdoğan characterized protesters as "çapulcu" (looters/vagabonds) and used the protests to justify security crackdowns. Hundreds arrested over subsequent years. The "red dress" activist and others prosecuted. Structural democratic decline continued; 2016 coup attempt accelerated authoritarian consolidation.
intervention_levers: International solidarity, documenting repression, protecting prosecuted activists, connecting to broader European democratic standards, diaspora organizing.

name: Colombia: Marea Verde — Biodiversity Conservation
issue: Petro government's commitment to transition from oil extraction; biodiversity protection in Amazon/Chocó.

ncrs.rationale: COP16 achieved record civil society participation and new biodiversity financing mechanisms. Colombia's hosting created visual opportunity — extreme biodiversity in accessible location combined with treaty moment.

trigger: COP16 Cali (October 2024): extraordinary civil society mobilization and an agreement on biodiversity financing that included new language on indigenous territorial rights.
outcome: COP16 achieved record civil society participation, new biodiversity financing mechanisms, and a historic agreement on digital sequence information sharing from biodiversity. Implementation remains the challenge.
intervention_levers: COP summit timing, indigenous rights framing, biodiversity visualization, corporate engagement, implementation monitoring, connecting to broader climate and rights frameworks.

name: Bhopal Disaster — Ongoing Accountability
issue: Union Carbide/Dow Chemical's continuing accountability for 1984 gas leak killing 3,500-15,000 immediately and 500,000+ long-term health impacts.

ncrs.rationale: 40 years of corporate impunity despite complete evidence. Site still contaminated. Dow/BASF maintain 'no liability.' Longest-running corporate accountability campaign demonstrates need for legal enforcement infrastructure, not just moral argument.

trigger: Multiple recurring triggers: anniversary marches, each documented birth defect, International Chemical Weapons Day. The 1989 settlement trigger: its inadequacy ($470M for 500,000 victims) was itself an injustice that motivated subsequent organizing.
outcome: No adequate compensation. No criminal accountability. Site still contaminated. Dow Chemical/BASF (acquired Dow) maintain they have "no liability." The longest-running corporate accountability campaign demonstrates both the endurance possible in survivor-led movements and the power of corporate impunity without legal enforcement.
intervention_levers: Anniversary mobilization, site remediation demands, extradition requests, shareholder activism at Dow/BASF, connecting to broader corporate accountability movement, documentary filmmaking.

name: Thailand: Save the Ocean / Lanta Campaign
issue: Marine plastic pollution in Thailand's Andaman Sea and Gulf of Thailand; corporate accountability for plastic producers.

ncrs.rationale: Marium the dugong became national celebrity; death showed plastic waste in autopsy. Thai government banned single-use plastic bags January 2020. Named individual animals create emotional connections statistics cannot achieve.

trigger: Marium's death, August 2019. She had become a national celebrity after being rescued. Her death was documented in detail. The autopsy showed plastic waste.
outcome: Thai government banned single-use plastic bags in major retail stores January 1, 2020. Accelerated by Marium coverage combined with international plastic ban trends. Limited to bags; broader plastic regulation slower.
intervention_levers: Named individual animals as protagonists, social media following before death, autopsy documentation, connecting to broader plastic campaigns, corporate retail targeting.

name: Global Anti-Dam Movement / Narmada
issue: Sardar Sarovar Dam on the Narmada River displacing 320,000+ indigenous and tribal people without compensation.

ncrs.rationale: World Bank withdrew. But India built dam anyway with national funding. Movement established World Bank accountability mechanisms (inspection panel), inspired global anti-dam movement, but couldn't stop the dam. Jal samarpan (choosing to drown) was extraordinary moral witness.

trigger: 1993: World Bank Independent Review (Morse Commission) found the dam was not in compliance with the Bank's own policies — institutional self-indictment. Combined with sustained pressure, World Bank withdrew from the project.
outcome: World Bank withdrew. But: India built the dam anyway with national funding. Displacement occurred. Supreme Court of India upheld dam; raised height multiple times. Movement's victory was partial: established World Bank accountability mechanisms (inspection panel), inspired global anti-dam movement, but couldn't stop the dam.
intervention_levers: World Bank accountability, international solidarity, Narmada Bachao Andolan organization, hunger strikes, legal challenges, jal samarpan as extreme witness, documentary filmmaking.
slogans: Doobenge lekin hatenge nahin (We'll drown but won't move)

name: Wangari Maathai: Karura Forest Defense
issue: Government allocation of public forest land for private development; environmental governance accountability.

ncrs.rationale: Karura Forest protected. Public land allocations reversed. Contributed to Moi government's political crisis and eventual democratic transition. Tree planting as civil disobedience created perfect visual argument when met with violence.

trigger: January 8, 1999: Maathai and supporters entered Karura Forest to plant trees in construction-cleared areas. Police and private security attacked them — Maathai and supporters beaten on camera. International coverage followed.
outcome: Public land allocations in Karura reversed. Forest protected. Campaign contributed to Moi government's broader political crisis. Part of the long-arc process that eventually led to Kenya's 2002 democratic transition (Moi did not run again).
intervention_levers: Tree planting as civil disobedience, international media coverage, Nobel Prize recognition (later), connecting to broader democratic reform, documenting violence against peaceful protesters.

name: Uganda: Stop Kony / KONY 2012
issue: Joseph Kony and Lord's Resistance Army's crimes; demands for ICC capture.

ncrs.rationale: 100 million views in 6 days — fastest video to reach that mark. 'Cover the Night' largely failed. Director's breakdown derailed campaign. Kony never captured. 'Slacktivism' critique entered discourse. Audience engagement ≠ policy pressure — mechanism from view to action was fatally underdesigned.

trigger: March 5, 2012: "KONY 2012" video released on YouTube. 100 million views in 6 days — fastest video to 100 million in history at that point.
outcome: "Cover the Night" (April 20) largely failed — minimal participation despite massive online engagement. Director Jason Russell had a public breakdown in March 2012 that derailed the campaign. "Slacktivism" debate entered mainstream discourse. Kony never captured. Invisible Children faced criticism from Ugandan activists for paternalism.
intervention_levers: Viral video production, but needed clearer action mechanism, local partnership, realistic goals, organizational resilience, connecting views to policy pressure.

name: Indonesia: Peatland Fires / Haze Crisis Accountability
issue: Annual burning of peatlands (many by palm oil companies) causing catastrophic haze across Malaysia, Singapore, Philippines.

ncrs.rationale: No-burn pledges from major companies. RSPO standards tightened. Indonesia passed new laws. 2019 fires similar scale suggests inadequate structural change. Corporate pressure generated commitments but implementation weak.

trigger: 2015: The extreme scale of fires (half a billion tons of CO2 emitted — equal to Germany's annual emissions in 3 months) made avoidance impossible. Singapore's "haze" hit Hazardous levels for weeks.
outcome: No-burn pledges by major palm oil companies and buyers. RSPO (Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil) standards tightened. Indonesia passed new laws; enforcement uneven. 2019 fires similar scale to 2015 — suggesting inadequate structural change. The corporate pressure succeeded in generating commitments but implementation lagged.
intervention_levers: Corporate no-burn pledges, RSPO standards, satellite monitoring, Singapore/Malaysia diplomatic pressure, consumer campaigns, connecting to climate emissions.

name: Mexico: Women's Movement and Day of Absence
issue: Epidemic of femicide and gender violence; state impunity for perpetrators.

ncrs.rationale: Largest women's marches in Mexican history. Day of Absence made women's absence visible. AMLO dismissed movement. No immediate legislative change but femicide tracking improved. 'If we stop, the world stops' demonstrated through negative space.

trigger: February 9, 2020: Murder of 25-year-old Ingrid Escamilla — body mutilated and photographed; photos published on newspaper front pages. Public outrage at both the murder and the sensationalist coverage. March 9: nationwide Day of Absence ('Un Día Sin Nosotras').
outcome: No immediate legislative change. López Obrador dismissed the movement. But: femicide rates began to be tracked more carefully; "feminist emergency" declared in several Mexican states. The Day of Absence as tactical innovation spread — adopted in other countries. The feminist movement's organizational infrastructure strengthened.
intervention_levers: Day of Absence tactic, marches, counting femicides, media accountability, electoral pressure, connecting to regional feminist movements, protecting feminist activists.
slogans: If we stop, the world stops

name: Occupy Hong Kong 2014 — Umbrella Movement
issue: Beijing's decision to pre-screen candidates for Hong Kong's 2017 Chief Executive election; demand for genuine universal suffrage.

ncrs.rationale: 79-day occupation ended without concessions. Beijing did not change framework. Most visually beautiful protest space of decade — art, study areas, Lennon Walls. But built networks that reemerged in 2019. Visual creativity insufficient against authoritarian state power.

trigger: August 31, 2014: China's Standing Committee announced its decision on election framework — pre-screening of candidates. Protest erupted. September 28: police tear-gassed protesters; spontaneous occupation of major roads followed.
outcome: Occupation lasted 79 days; ended without concessions. Beijing did not change framework. The 2014 movement failed completely on its demands. But it built the organizational networks that reemerged in 2019.
intervention_levers: Umbrella as symbol, occupation infrastructure, Lennon Walls, international solidarity, legal defense, connecting to Hong Kong identity, building networks for future mobilization.

name: Hong Kong 2019
issue: Extradition Bill allowing removal of Hong Kong residents to mainland China; broader demand for democratic accountability.

ncrs.rationale: 2 million marched (27% of population). Extradition Bill withdrawn — tactical win. Then National Security Law imposed June 2020. Mass arrests, organizations dissolved, leaders imprisoned. Hong Kong's democratic character destroyed. Perfect conditions (46/60) still lost to state security apparatus.

trigger: March-June 2019: escalating protests. June 9: 1 million marched (city of 7.4 million). June 12: police fired rubber bullets. June 16: 2 million marched — largest protest in Hong Kong history, approximately 27% of the entire population.
outcome: Extradition Bill withdrawn (September 2019) — tactical win. But: National Security Law (NSL) imposed June 30, 2020. Mass arrests of activists. Major pro-democracy organizations dissolved. Legislators, journalists, activists imprisoned. Hong Kong's democratic character fundamentally destroyed. The movement won its immediate demand then was crushed anyway.
intervention_levers: International solidarity, Magnitsky-style sanctions, supporting diaspora and exile activists, documentation for future accountability, protecting refugees, maintaining memory.
slogans: 光復香港 時代革命

name: Russia Anti-War Movement
issue: Opposition to Putin's invasion of Ukraine; anti-militarist sentiment among Russian urban educated class.

ncrs.rationale: 16,000+ arrested in first weeks. 'Discrediting the military' law imposed 15 years imprisonment. Navalny died in prison. No visible organized movement survived. Below minimum threshold — without permission structure (score 1) and conformity trap broken, movements cannot form openly.

trigger: February 24, 2022: Invasion of Ukraine. Spontaneous anti-war protests in Moscow, St. Petersburg. Thousands arrested in first days.
outcome: 16,000+ arrested in first weeks. Virtually all visible protest suppressed. "Discrediting the military" law imposed 15 years imprisonment for anti-war speech. Navalny died in prison February 2024. No anti-war movement survived in Russia as visible organized force. Many anti-war Russians emigrated ("Relocation").
intervention_levers: Limited in authoritarian context. Supporting exile organizations, information access (VPN, alternative media), documenting for future accountability, sanctions targeting specific individuals.

name: US No Kings Anti-Trump Resistance
issue: Trump second term's democratic backsliding, dismantling of regulatory agencies, and authoritarian consolidation.

ncrs.rationale: Sustained mobilization at historically high levels. Courts blocking multiple executive actions. Compound crisis tip — each new cut expands coalition. Highest-scored active situation in 2025-2026. Whether this translates to 2026 election outcomes remains to be seen.

trigger: Early 2025: Multiple simultaneous triggers — federal employee layoffs, NIH grant terminations hitting universities and hospitals, immigration enforcement in communities, Medicaid cuts affecting specific families. Compound crisis mobilization.
outcome: As of March 2026: sustained mobilization at historically high levels. Courts have blocked multiple executive actions. Several Republican senators have broken with Trump on specific issues. Policy rollbacks slower than administration's intentions. Whether this tip translates to 2026 election outcomes remains to be seen.
intervention_levers: Coalition-building across affected groups, court challenges, state-level resistance, electoral organizing, protecting vulnerable communities, documenting impacts, connecting specific harms to administration actions.

name: Sudan Revolution Phase II — Civil War
issue: Democratic transition collapses; military factions (SAF vs. RSF) go to war; civilian population caught between two armies.

ncrs.rationale: 12 million displaced, 150,000+ killed. Most catastrophic 'after the revolution' failure. 2019 revolution's gains completely destroyed. Demonstrates what happens when revolution wins but can't build institutions fast enough.

trigger: April 15, 2023: SAF and RSF began fighting each other in Khartoum. The two military factions who had cooperated to destroy democracy then fought for sole control.
outcome: As of 2025: 12 million displaced (second largest displacement crisis globally). 150,000+ killed. Famine in multiple regions. No ceasefire. The 2019 revolution's gains completely destroyed. Sudan is the most catastrophic "after the revolution" failure in the dataset.
intervention_levers: Humanitarian aid, ceasefire diplomacy, arms embargo, accountability documentation, supporting civilian resistance committees, preparing for post-conflict transition.

name: New Zealand: Zero Carbon Act / Jacinda Ardern's Leadership
issue: Legislating a binding net-zero emissions pathway; climate governance as moral leadership.

ncrs.rationale: Zero Carbon Act passed 119-1 — extraordinary cross-party support. Climate Change Commission established. Subsequent governments maintained core commitments. Model studied globally. Demonstrates deliberate institution-building can create durable change.

trigger: Zero Carbon Act legislative process 2018-2019. The bill achieved extraordinary cross-party support (119-1) because Ardern's government spent two years building genuine consensus (Zero Carbon Act Committee included all parties).
outcome: Zero Carbon Act passed November 2019. Climate Change Commission established. Subsequent governments (including National) have maintained core commitments despite political changes. Model studied globally as example of durable, cross-party climate legislation. New Zealand demonstrates that deliberate institution-building can create durable change.
intervention_levers: Cross-party consensus building, independent commission structure, phased implementation, building durability into law design, international model sharing.

name: Tanzania: Mama Mia / Women Land Rights
issue: Women's land rights in Tanzania's customary law context; documentation and legal protection.

ncrs.rationale: No single tipping point but sustained 20-year campaign with accumulating gains. 'Long game' model — documentation + legal + community organizing. Demonstrates that some campaigns require steady pressure over decades rather than dramatic tips.

trigger: No single trigger — ongoing campaign using documentation, legal advocacy, and community mobilization. The 2015 Land Acquisition Bill proposed to further restrict land rights, creating a mobilization window.
outcome: Women's land rights protections strengthened in some regions through legal clinics and awareness campaigns. Not a single tipping point but a sustained 20-year campaign with accumulating gains. The model of parallel documentation + legal + community organizing is the toolkit most applicable to slow violence campaigns.
intervention_levers: Legal clinics, community paralegals, documentation of land tenure, connecting to international land rights frameworks, women's organizing, policy advocacy.

name: Tajikistan/Central Asia: Water Rights and Climate Displacement
issue: Shrinking Aral Sea and glacial melt creating water scarcity, agricultural collapse, and forced displacement.

ncrs.rationale: No political tipping. Crisis continues. Authoritarian governance prevents civil society response. Pure 'slow violence without visibility' case — conditions for tip don't exist because permission structure is absent. Visual documentation IS the missing ingredient.

trigger: No trigger has produced a political tipping — authoritarian governance in most countries prevents organized civil society response. Kyrgyzstan has the most open political context.
outcome: No political tipping. The crisis continues. International aid programs exist but do not address root causes (Soviet irrigation infrastructure, regional water governance failure, climate change acceleration). This is a pure "slow violence without visibility" case — the conditions for a tip don't exist because the permission structure is absent.
intervention_levers: Visual documentation, international climate adaptation funding, regional water governance diplomacy, supporting civil society in more open contexts (Kyrgyzstan), connecting to global climate justice.

name: Global: Digital Rights / Edward Snowden Revelations
issue: NSA/GCHQ mass surveillance of global internet communications revealed; digital rights and privacy as human rights.

ncrs.rationale: USA FREEDOM Act reformed some NSA collection. End-to-end encryption became mainstream. GDPR partly influenced by post-Snowden discourse. 'Document everything in advance' model — 18 months preparation, staged releases maximized impact.

trigger: June 5, 2013: Guardian publishes PRISM program revelations. June 9: Snowden identifies himself. Subsequent weeks: revelations about XKeyscore, MUSCULAR, Tempora (UK), mass collection of phone metadata. Staged release strategy maximized attention.
outcome: USA FREEDOM Act (2015) reformed some NSA collection. End-to-end encryption became mainstream (WhatsApp, Signal adoption). Tech companies invested in encryption. EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR 2018) partly influenced by post-Snowden data rights discourse. No criminal accountability for NSA violations; Snowden remains in exile.
intervention_levers: Journalist partnerships, staged document release, technical community engagement, encryption adoption campaigns, legislative advocacy, connecting to broader civil liberties framework.

name: Climate Justice: Loss and Damage — Vanuatu ICJ Advisory Opinion Campaign
issue: International Court of Justice advisory opinion on states' obligations to protect climate system; legal framework for climate reparations.

ncrs.rationale: 132-nation coalition built on moral clarity, not power. ICJ advisory opinion expected 2025-2026. Would provide foundation for climate reparations, loss and damage financing, future litigation globally. Small vulnerable states leveraged moral authority into disproportionate impact.

trigger: April 29, 2024: UN General Assembly adopted resolution requesting ICJ advisory opinion on climate obligations. September 2024: Hearings at The Hague. 98 countries and 9 international organizations presented arguments — largest participation in ICJ history.
outcome: ICJ advisory opinion expected 2025-2026. While not binding, an ICJ opinion on climate obligations would be the most authoritative legal statement on the issue ever made — providing the foundation for climate reparations arguments, loss and damage financing, and future litigation globally. The 132-nation coalition was built on moral clarity, not power.
intervention_levers: Small state coalition diplomacy, Pacific Island leadership, youth engagement, legal expertise, connecting to broader climate litigation, ICJ hearing participation, amplifying frontline voices.
